perturbation.original_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Board of Directors will determine the amount of any extra payment. It will be based on each member's fair share of the deficiency. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a payment in cash. If you do not make the extra payment within 60 days from the date we notify you of your share of the deficiency, the amount will be charged as an indebtedness against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 5% per year.
perturbation.changed_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Executive Committee will determine the amount of any additional due. It will be based on each member's contribution ratio. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a contribution in kind. If you do not provide the supplemental payment within 90 days from the date we inform you of your portion of the shortfall, the amount will be considered a debt against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 7% per year.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces several contradictions. 'Board of Directors' is changed to 'Executive Committee', 'extra payment' to 'additional due', 'member's fair share of the deficiency' to 'member's contribution ratio', 'payment in cash' to 'contribution in kind', '60 days' to '90 days', 'notify' to 'inform', 'share of the deficiency' to 'portion of the shortfall', and 'indebtedness' to 'debt', and '5%' to '7%'. This contradicts the initial definition of the solvency process, creating confusion about who determines the amount, how it's calculated, the form payment can take, and the conditions under which the company will determine an extra cost and when it can be charged against the contract and the interest rate on that amount. It is not clear that any of these new terms are synonymous.
perturbation.contradicted_text: MAINTENANCE OF SOLVENCY. This provision applies only to benefits provided through the General Account. If the solvency of the Society becomes impaired, you may be required to make an extra payment. The Executive Committee will determine the amount of any additional due. It will be based on each member's contribution ratio. You may make the extra payment by an equivalent reduction in benefits or by a contribution in kind. If you do not provide the supplemental payment within 90 days from the date we inform you of your portion of the shortfall, the amount will be considered a debt against the contract with interest compounded at the rate of 7% per year.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces misaligned terminology ('Board of Directors' to 'Executive Committee', 'extra payment' to 'additional due', 'member's fair share of the deficiency' to 'member's contribution ratio', 'payment in cash' to 'contribution in kind', '60 days' to '90 days', 'notify' to 'inform', 'share of the deficiency' to 'portion of the shortfall', and 'indebtedness' to 'debt', and '5%' to '7%') without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations. This creates a strong in-text contradiction by altering key aspects of the solvency maintenance process, such as who determines the payment amount, how it's calculated, acceptable payment forms, the notification timeline, and the interest rate, directly conflicting with the contradicted text (which is the same text after modification). According to the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction', this leads to confusion and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The person(s) named as the Insured(s) or the Annuitant(s) is a benefit member of the Society. Rights and privileges of membership are set forth in the Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society. These rights and privileges are separate from the ownership of this contract.
perturbation.changed_text: The individual(s) designated as the Policyholder(s) or the Recipient(s) is a stakeholder of the Organization. Entitlements and benefits of affiliation are detailed in the Charter and Regulations of the Enterprise. These entitlements and benefits are contingent on the possession of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation replaces 'person(s)' with 'individual(s)', 'Insured(s) or the Annuitant(s)' with 'Policyholder(s) or the Recipient(s)', 'benefit member of the Society' with 'stakeholder of the Organization', 'Rights and privileges of membership' with 'Entitlements and benefits of affiliation', 'Articles of Incorporation and Bylaws of the Society' with 'Charter and Regulations of the Enterprise' and 'separate from the ownership of this contract' with 'contingent on the possession of this agreement'. It is unclear whether these terms are interchangeable. This creates an in-text contradiction, generating uncertainty about who holds membership rights and how they relate to contract ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: MEMBERSHIP. The individual(s) designated as the Policyholder(s) or the Recipient(s) is a stakeholder of the Organization. Entitlements and benefits of affiliation are detailed in the Charter and Regulations of the Enterprise. These entitlements and benefits are contingent on the possession of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that rights and privileges of membership are separate from the ownership of the contract. However, the changed text asserts that 'entitlements and benefits of affiliation are contingent on the possession of this agreement'. This creates a direct contradiction because one states that membership rights are independent of contract ownership, while the other states that certain entitlements are dependent on contract ownership. Furthermore, both the changed text and the contradicted text are verbatim, indicating that the change was meant to overwrite the clause elsewhere in the document. This change directly contradicts the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Cash. Dividends are paid in cash. Payment of Premium. Dividends are applied as payment of a Net Premium.
perturbation.changed_text: Cash. Dividends are paid in currency. Payment of Premium. Dividends are utilized as remittance for a Reduced Premium.
perturbation.explanation: Here, 'cash' is changed to 'currency', 'applied as payment' to 'utilized as remittance' and 'Net Premium' to 'Reduced Premium'. While seemingly similar, these terms introduce ambiguity. Specifically, it's unclear if 'currency' is limited to physical cash or includes electronic transfers, and whether a 'Reduced Premium' is the same as a 'Net Premium', especially considering the prior definition. This leads to potential disputes over the acceptable forms of dividend payment and how they affect the premium amount. This is an in-text contradiction because it alters the meaning of how payments are executed and accounted for, potentially affecting the financial obligations of both parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: DIVIDEND OPTIONS. If dividends are credited after premiums can no longer be paid under this contract, dividends will be paid in cash. Otherwise, dividends will be applied under the Payment of Premium option unless the Cash option has been chosen in writing. Cash. Dividends are paid in currency. Payment of Premium. Dividends are utilized as remittance for a Reduced Premium.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies key financial terms. The original defined 'cash' and 'Net Premium' clearly. The changed text introduces 'currency' and 'Reduced Premium,' creating ambiguity. The contradicted section repeats both the original and modified terms, highlighting the inconsistency. This directly contradicts the initial defined payment methods, creating potential disputes and uncertainty over which payment terms apply, fulfilling the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.
